title: Patching a lath-and-plaster wall
subtitle: Repair a broken section of traditional lime-plaster wall without cracking the surrounding coat
excerpt: Traditional lath-and-plaster walls require different repair techniques from modern plasterboard: the key steps are stabilising the laths, bridging the gap with scrim, building up in two coats of lime-compatible basecoat, and finishing to match the texture of the original surface.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional Building Skills syllabus (SPAB). Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: are found in houses built before approximately 1950. The three-coat lime plaster system is harder than gypsum and moves more than modern board does; patching with standard lightweight filler leads to cracking at the edges within months because the materials expand and contract at different rates. The repair needs to follow the original structure: stabilise the laths, key the edges, build up in two coats, and match the original texture.

Tap the surrounding plaster gently with a knuckle. Hollow-sounding sections have separated from the laths. These sections are not structural; they will not fail immediately, but they will crack around the repair patch. Either confine the repair strictly to the broken section, or extend the cut-out to sound plaster on all sides. Cutting back to sound plaster takes longer but produces a lasting repair.
Step 1, cut back to sound plaster and expose the laths

Cut the edge of the patch cleanly with a utility knife or cold chisel, undercutting the edge slightly so the new plaster can grip behind the old surface. Remove all loose and hollow plaster from the repair area. Brush out any dust and debris from between the laths. If any laths are broken, cut them back to the nearest stud on each side and fix short new lath sections using 30 mm nails, leaving 8 to 10 mm gaps between lath strips.
Step 2, stabilise the perimeter and key the surface

Apply a coat of PVA bonding agent (diluted 1 part PVA to 5 parts water) to the cut edges of the old plaster and allow it to become tacky before plastering. Self-adhesive scrim tape bedded across the junction between old plaster and new lath area bridges the join and prevents the crack from tracking back to the surface.

to a thick, creamy consistency. Apply with the trowel to the lath area, pressing the plaster firmly through the gaps between the laths to form a key on the back. Build up to approximately 10 mm below the finished surface level. This is the equivalent of the traditional
. Scratch the surface with a comb or serrated tool while still wet. Allow to set and firm up (typically 2 hours) but not to full hardness before applying the second coat.

Apply multi-finish plaster over the bonding coat, filling the area flush with the surrounding surface. Work the trowel in arcs across the plaster, keeping the blade nearly flat. As the plaster begins to set (the surface changes from shiny to matt), firm up with the trowel using light, even passes. Dampen the trowel slightly to polish the surface, but do not over-work it: gypsum finish trowelled too late tears and pits. If the surrounding original surface has a textured or stippled finish, match it with a sponge or brush before the plaster fully sets.
Step 5, allow to dry and harden before decorating

New plaster must dry fully before it can be painted. In a heated room allow at least 1 week for a 15 mm deep repair; deeper repairs need up to 4 weeks depending on ventilation. New plaster is mid-grey when wet and turns a uniform pink-to-white as it dries and sets. Paint applied over wet plaster blisters and peels. Apply a mist coat (emulsion diluted 20 per cent with water) as the first coat over the fully dried repair area.

attrs.fix: Cut the repair out again to sound plaster, bridge the junction with scrim tape, and re-apply. The scrim distributes stress across the joint.
attrs.cause: The perimeter was not keyed or bridged with scrim; old plaster and new plaster expand and contract at slightly different rates.
attrs.symptom: Crack appears at the join between old and new plaster within weeks of decorating

attrs.fix: Dampen the laths with clean water before applying the first coat. Mix the plaster slightly stiffer.
attrs.cause: Plaster applied too wet; the laths were not dampened before plastering (dry laths pull moisture out of the plaster too fast).
attrs.symptom: First coat falls off the laths before setting

attrs.fix: Allow the bonding coat to become firm (2 hours minimum) before applying the finish. If the surface is still rough after drying, sand with fine sandpaper (180-grit) before misting and decorating.
attrs.cause: Multi-finish used on top of a bonding coat that had not set sufficiently; the bonding coat continued to stiffen and pulled moisture from the finish coat.
attrs.symptom: Finish coat dries with a rough or sandy texture
